text: with few windows. Ronin approached the counter, built immediately outside one of the doors. The scrapper was a tall, thin synth who’d worn the skin off his hands and never deigned to replace it. Considering his profession, it wouldn’t have lasted long, anyway . “Dustwalker. I detected the clang of your pack a mile out.” Zeke’s modulator produced a voice that was deep and rough, somehow perfectly fitted to Cheyenne . Ronin unslung his rifle to swing his pack off his shoulders. Unclasping the flap, he loosened the drawstring and upended the bag. Scrap clattered out onto the scuffed and scratched counter. Zeke watched, expression neutral, as bundles of tangled copper wire bounced over plates of lead and steel, as plastic chips and long-dead power cells clattered over the surface. Plunging a hand into his pocket, Ronin added the ammunition he’d taken from the reavers to the pile . “Never seen one as productive as you,” Zeke said. His hands hovered over the pile, fingers twitching. “You’re a far-rover, to be sure. Creators programmed you special .” “How much?” Ronin asked. His core programming was shrouded within a deep, corrupted memory bank. If the Creators had instilled him with a special purpose, they’d also gone out of their way to hide it from him; he was just another bot with no discernable directive . Zeke sifted through the pile, rubbing and tapping various items and occasionally lifting a piece to test its weight . “Forty units advance. Give me an hour, and I’ll have your full tally .” “ Estimate .” The middle and little fingers on Zeke’s right hand curled for an instant, spasmed, and straightened. “Three fifty. Depends on the damage to the cells, and the precious in the chips .” Nodding, Ronin slung his rucksack over one shoulder and his rifle over the other. Would the woman dance for hard credit ? Zeke counted out the chits — plastic discs with Warlord’s symbol etched at the center, grooves radiating outward from it like spokes on a wheel — and stacked them on the counter. Two yellows and a green made forty units . Ronin slid them off the counter and dropped them into his pocket. Credit units were well enough for now, but they wouldn’t hold any value outside Cheyenne. He’d have to convert them into solid goods before he moved on to another town . “One hour,” he said, walking away from the scrapper’s . He ran his optics over the Market, noting the presence of the merchants he’d need to visit. Forty credits wouldn’t get him much — ammunition was rare, and therefore expensive — and he didn’t care to negotiate prices without the chits in hand . Kitty’s gaudy lights caught his attention again. The recording of the woman dancing rose to the forefront of his attention. An hour spent sating his curiosity couldn’t be considered wasted time. Perhaps it’d be long enough to understand why the redhaired woman had so intrigued him. He walked toward the building . Perhaps it was simply that he’d been in the Dust too long. Diagnostics checks told him his processors were functioning normally, that there was no new corrupted data, but how could he be certain ? It was always his choice to go out into the wasteland and scavenge, at immense risk to his functionality. Often, he was forced to fight. Rarely did those fights end without Ronin having sustained some sort of damage. Yet it was only in those instances, in the chaotic din of combat, that he felt closest to fulfilling his core programming. It had been the same for one hundred and eighty-five years . Why now? Why had the woman’s dance invaded his thoughts so thoroughly ? He stopped at the front door, staring up at the neon sign. He’d not once been tempted to enter before tonight . But change was a natural part of existence for all things. Even mountains changed, infinitesimally, over the eons. Why not bots, as well ? Ronin opened the door and stepped inside. There was a partial wall directly ahead, creating a small foyer and dulling the rhythmic thump of music from beyond. The space was dominated by a broad, blocky bot, at least nine feet tall. Its twin optics audibly shifted to focus on Ronin as the bot raised its thick arms and folded them over its dented metal chest. Warlord’s symbol was clear on its left shoulder, in fresh red paint . Though the source of the knowledge was unclear, Ronin knew this bot had been made for warfare . “Ten units to watch,” it said, voice projecting from somewhere within its suspension-cable neck. It had no moving mouth . One frivolous expenditure couldn’t hurt. It might mean a few less bullets, but he needed to be conservative with ammunition, anyway. Ronin plucked a yellow chit from his pocket and dropped it into the bot’s waiting hand . The bot curled its fingers over the chit. “You the dustwalker the boss mentioned ?” “I’m a dustwalker,” Ronin replied. “Couldn’t tell you which one he meant .” It lifted its head, optic shutters curling small, and released an electronic grunt. “I’m Comp . You — ” “ Comp ?” “Yeah. Short for Compactor. You start trouble, you deal with me. You don’t want that .” “Astute observation .” Comp grunted again and extended a thumb, jabbing it to the side. Ronin walked around the foyer, giving Comp a wide berth, and entered the main room . It took two seconds for his optics to adjust to the confused lighting. More pink and violet bulbs cast conflicting glows upon numerous reflective surfaces, strengthening the contrasting gloom hanging in the air. Mirrors and polished chrome poles and rails gleamed around the room — on the stage, on the bar, around the doors, even on the ceiling . More so than the Bot District, this place was a blatant statement of defiance against the Dust. A callback
